analysis.title: Decompose core implementation into cohesive modules

change_surface.import_references_to_update: 50+ across wizard.py, bench.py, 8 engine files, 8+ test files

analysis.architecture_summary: core.py (4,785 lines) is a God Module containing URL/port constants, shell utilities, 6 RuntimeAdapter classes (Ollama/LMStudio/LlamaCpp/VLLM/Router9/OpenRouter), all engine-specific probe/smoke functions, HuggingFace API wrappers, llmfit bridge, llamacpp server lifecycle, machine profiling, model selection, and doctor CLI. The engines/ directory already demonstrates the modular pattern core.py should follow. 19 downstream files import from core as `pb` or `core`.

options.name: Minimal extraction of self-contained helpers
options.summary: Extract only the most self-contained helpers (constants, shell utilities, HF API, llmfit bridge) into separate modules. Keep RuntimeAdapter classes, model selection, machine profiling, and llama-server lifecycle in core.py. Update imports across the codebase.

files_to_modify.changes: Remove constants, shell utils, HF API, llmfit — leaving ~3,200 lines of adapters, model selection, profiling, server lifecycle. Update remaining internal imports.
Update imports: replace `pb.CONSTANT` references with module-qualified names for extracted items.
Update imports for extracted items.
Update imports for extracted constants/shell utils.
Update imports for extracted items.
Update imports for extracted items.
Update imports for extracted items.
Update import paths for extracted items; add new test files for extracted modules.
Update import paths (7+ additional test files).

files_to_create.purpose: URL/port constants and default settings extracted from core.py
Shell command utilities (run_subprocess, etc.) extracted from core.py
HuggingFace API wrappers extracted from core.py

options.test_strategy: Extract corresponding test cases from test_core_unit.py into new test__config.py, test__shell.py, test__hf_api.py, test__llmfit.py. Existing adapter/model selection/server tests remain in test_core_unit.py untouched. Run full existing test suite to confirm no regressions.
pros: Low risk — minimal change to the most stable parts of core.py
No risk to adapter/model selection/server logic — those stay untouched
cons: core.py remains a God Module at ~3,200 lines with 5+ unrelated concerns
Adapters, model selection, profiling, server lifecycle stayed tangled
Does not align with the engines/* modular pattern already established
Limited future evolution benefit — most of the coupling remains
options.risk_details: Extracted items are self-contained helper functions/constants with no internal coupling to adapters or model selection. Test extraction is straightforward. Low blast radius if something goes wrong.

options.name: Balanced full decomposition with compatibility facade
options.summary: Extract ALL major concerns from core.py into separate dedicated modules. Keep core.py as a backward-compatible re-export facade so all 19 downstream files continue to work without import changes. This is the recommended approach — it achieves full separation while maintaining zero-disruption imports.

files_to_modify.changes: Replace all ~4,785 lines with a compatibility facade that re-exports every public symbol from the new sub-modules. Add deprecation notices (optional).
Optionally update imports to point to new modules directly (not required — facade preserves `pb.*`).
Optionally expose new module paths in package-level __init__.py.

files_to_create.purpose: All 6 RuntimeAdapter classes and the abstract base
Model selection logic and decision tree
GGUF file scanning and metadata extraction

options.test_strategy: Split test_core_unit.py (3,451 lines) into per-module test files matching each new module (test__config.py, test__adapters.py, etc.). Keep test fixture/data-sharing via conftest.py. Run full suite to verify facade re-exports match original symbols exactly. Test files importing `core as pb` continue working without changes.
pros: Full architectural separation — each concern can evolve independently
Zero import disruption — 19 downstream files need zero changes (facade pattern)
Aligns with the existing engines/* modular pattern
Enables focused, smaller test files per module
Clear ownership boundaries for future contributors
Facade can issue deprecation warnings for future direct imports
cons: Facade maintenance overhead — must stay in sync with new modules
Module-level mutable constants (e.g., OLLAMA_BASE_URL) need careful handling when reassigned by wizard.py for remote endpoints — facade must forward reassignments
options.risk_details: Main risk is subtle import-order issues and module-level mutable constant forwarding. The facade must ensure that reassignment of `core.OLLAMA_BASE_URL` propagates to the backing `_config.OLLAMA_BASE_URL`. This can be solved by having `core` not re-export scalars by value but instead re-export the module itself or use a singleton config object. Medium risk because the facade pattern is well-understood and testable.

options.name: Comprehensive refactor with adapter migration and deprecation shims
options.summary: Same full decomposition as option 2, but additionally refactor the 6 RuntimeAdapter classes into per-engine modules (aligning with engines/*), add explicit deprecation warnings on old import paths, and create backward-compat __init__.py shims in the package root.

files_to_modify.changes: Replace with compatibility facade re-exporting from new modules and per-engine adapter modules.
Add lazy imports from new modules for common public symbols.
Replace with per-engine imports or remove entirely; adapters move to engines/*
Add RuntimeAdapter export (or create adapter module)
Add RuntimeAdapter export
Add RuntimeAdapter export
Add RuntimeAdapter export
Add RuntimeAdapter export
Add RuntimeAdapter export
Update imports to use new per-engine adapter paths (e.g., `from claude_codex_local.engines.ollama import OllamaAdapter`).
Update imports for adapter references.
Split entirely; adapter tests migrate to per-engine test files.

options.test_strategy: Same as option 2 plus: create per-engine adapter tests in each engine's directory (or tests/test_engines/ollama/ etc.). Add integration tests verifying old import paths emit deprecation warnings but still function. Run full suite plus engine-specific e2e tests.
pros: Most thorough decomposition — each concern and each engine is fully independent
Adapters live alongside their engine lifecycle code (perfect alignment with engines/* pattern)
Deprecation warnings guide downstream consumers to new import paths
cons: Highest risk of breakage — 19 downstream files need import updates
Adapter migration may reveal hidden coupling between RuntimeAdapter classes and shared utilities currently in core.py
Requires careful coordination; best done as a feature branch with multiple PRs
More aggressive than needed for the immediate goal of enabling independent evolution
options.risk_details: Moving 6 RuntimeAdapter classes to per-engine modules risks breaking import chains; adapters may have undocumented cross-references or shared state. Deprecation shims add surface area. High blast radius: 19 downstream files across the codebase must update imports. Recommended only if the team has capacity for a dedicated refactoring sprint.
